q: The human nervous system is made up of which two main parts?
Which general region of the brain comprises the cerebellum, pons and medulla oblongata?
Which part of the brain acts as the centres for intellect, learning and memory?
Which brain structure acts as the 'great relay station' of the brain?
Which part of the brain helps to regulate the body's internal environment and contains neurones that control blood pressure and heart rate?
Which walnut-shaped structure is involved in the unconscious coordination of posture and reflexes?
The butterfly-shaped core of the spinal cord is made up of what?
How many pairs of cranial nerves are there in the somatic nervous system?
Which cranial nerve has branches to the throat, heart, lungs, and digestive tract?
Which nervous system controls internal reactions under automatic or involuntary control?
What are sudden, involuntary responses to certain stimuli called?
Approximately how long do reflexes take to occur?
Which neurotransmitter is released by sympathetic neurones in stressful situations?
Which neurotransmitter is used by the parasympathetic system to control organ responses when the body is calm?
What is the effect of the sympathetic nervous system on the heart rate?
What are the cells that support the neurones and provide a supporting framework for nervous tissue called?
What is the Greek meaning of the word 'glial'?
Which part of the neurone conducts impulses away from the cell body?
What is the fatty, insulating layer enclosed around the axons of some neurones called?
Which type of neurones transmit impulses from the sensory receptors to the central nervous system?
Which neurones are found entirely within the central nervous system and act as a link between sensory and motor neurones?
Which structural type of neurone is found in the inner ear, the retina of the eye, and the olfactory area of the brain?
What is the connection between two neurones, or a neurone and an effector, called?
How wide is the small gap called the synaptic cleft?
How long does it take for neurotransmitters to diffuse across the synapse to reach the postsynaptic neurone?
Which sacs release their contents into the synaptic cleft by exocytosis?
If the effect of a neurotransmitter is inhibitory, it results in what state of the postsynaptic membrane?
Which neurotransmitter is an example of an excitatory neurotransmitter that excites muscle cell membranes?
Excessive production of which neurotransmitter is linked to schizophrenia?
Inadequate amounts of which neurotransmitter in the brain synapses are linked to depression?
Which neurotransmitters act as natural painkillers in synapses in the brain?
Overproduction of which neurotransmitter is linked to high blood pressure, anxiety, and insomnia?
Which part of the brain has a specific region for vision?
What is the outermost, tough, fibrous and opaque coat of the eyeball?
Which transparent layer is continuous with the sclera and refracts light rays that enter the eye?
Which part of the eye contains a black pigment to absorb light and prevent internal reflection?
Which circular sheet of muscles contains pigments that give the eye its colour?
Which photoreceptor cells are sensitive under low light and responsible for vision at night?
What is the area where neurones leave the retina and form the optic nerve, containing no photoreceptors?
Which jelly-like substance in the vitreous chamber prevents the collapse of the eye?
